concepts.title: What "open" means — weights, data, code, license

beats.text: A model release is not one thing but four separable artifacts, and a model can be open on some axes while shut on others.
The four artifacts are the trained weights, the training data, the training code, and the license that governs all of them.
Weights are the load-bearing piece: with the file you can run the model, fine-tune it, inspect it, and keep it forever.
The widely misunderstood point is that open weights say nothing about open data; the training corpus is almost never released.
By the Open Source Initiative's 2024 definition most open large language models are open-weight, not genuinely open-source.
Truly data-open models like OLMo, Pythia, and the SmolLM family exist, but they remain the exception rather than the rule.

concepts.title: The closed frontier — GPT, Claude, Gemini

beats.text: The three labs that most often define the frontier ship their flagships as closed, API-only products you can never download.
You send tokens in and get tokens out, billed per token, with the weights kept behind someone else's service.
Closing the box buys the strongest models, a managed service, instant access to new versions, and a maintained feature set.
The renting cost is purely marginal: there is no fixed cost, but every token costs money and the bill scales without bound.

visual.note: p is blended price per token, n is monthly token volume

beats.text: Because the bill is linear in volume, costs grow indefinitely as you scale, even as per-token prices fall each year.
Closed describes the weights, not the whole enterprise: these labs publish model cards, system cards, and technical reports.

concepts.title: The open ecosystem — Llama, Mistral, DeepSeek, Qwen

beats.text: The open-weight world is no longer a poor cousin of the frontier; for many tasks it is competitive and often the default.
Four families anchor it: Meta's Llama, Mistral AI, DeepSeek, and Alibaba's Qwen, each with a different license posture.
Llama catalyzed the ecosystem under a custom community license; Mistral and recent Qwen releases ship under Apache-2.0.
Weights compound: a single base model spawns thousands of community fine-tunes, quantizations, and merges, becoming a platform.
Open weights set a price floor: a near-frontier free model stops any API from charging frontier prices for commodity work.
Open here still means open-weight, not open-data; all four families ship parameters and a license without the training corpus.
The license is the part teams skim and later regret; with open weights it travels with the file and governs what you build.

visual.sub: Four questions cover most of it

beats.text: Four questions matter: commercial use, redistribution, acceptable use, and clauses on training or operating at extreme scale.
Apache-2.0 and MIT permit commercial use unambiguously; research-only and non-commercial licenses forbid paid products entirely.

visual.label: permitted uses scored in the matrix

beats.text: An OSI-approved license like Apache-2.0 permits all six tracked uses, while the closed API permits only commercial use.
Meta's community license adds a famous clause requiring a separate agreement above 700 million monthly active users.

visual.label: monthly active users before the scale clause bites

beats.text: Open is not a synonym for no rules; always read the actual LICENSE file, since models in one family ship under different terms.

visual.sub: The family name does not tell you the terms

beats.text: The decision rarely turns on benchmark scores; it turns on four forces and which one your specific job cares about most.
Open wins on privacy, cost at high volume, and version control; closed wins on raw capability and low-volume iteration.
Self-hosting flips the cost shape into a large fixed cost plus a tiny marginal cost, the mirror of the pure-marginal API.

visual.note: the two cost curves cross at breakeven volume n*

beats.text: Below the breakeven volume renting is cheaper; above it, self-hosting wins and keeps winning as the fixed cost amortizes.
At three dollars per million tokens, a fifteen-hundred-dollar GPU node breaks even near five hundred million tokens monthly.
Privacy is the hard override: if data cannot leave your network, you self-host open weights regardless of everything else.

visual.sub: Then volume, frontier, and MLOps decide
